FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study Release 5.1, N=4,754 youth at baseline (ages 9-10) and year 4 (ages 12-15)

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone/SM variables: average daily video game hours, average daily social media hours, smartphone/smartwatch ownership; also problematic screen use measured via VGAQ (Video Game Addiction Questionnaire), SMAQ (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire), MPIQ (Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire)

FT_IC3.evidence: Independent linear mixed-effects regression models testing whether baseline individual differences in 4 domains (impulsivity, motivation, psychopathology, cognitive ability) predict ST and PSU outcomes at ages 12-15. Multiple inferential tests reported with coefficients for each domain.

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 3 reports β, SE, p-values for screen time associations (e.g., Video game ST: Urgency b=+.07, SE=0.01, p<.001; Social media ST: Urgency b=+.06, SE=0.01, p<.001; Smartphone/smartwatch: coefficient direction reported). Table 4 reports coefficients for PSU outcomes.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective/longitudinal analysis: independent linear mixed-effects models examining whether baseline trait measures predict future screen use from ages 9-10 to 12-15

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in JAACAP Open, Volume 3, Number 4, December 2025, pages 1259-1269. DOI: 10.1016/j.jaacop.2025.06.007

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does NOT apply. Although smartphone/SM variables are DVs in all analyses and analyses are prospective, criterion (b) fails: predictors include individual psychological/clinical characteristics (urgency, sensation-seeking, lack of planning, lack of perseverance, reward sensitivity, punishment sensitivity, externalizing, internalizing, cognitive ability), NOT only demographic/contextual variables. Per E16: individual-characteristic predictors (including impulsivity, reward sensitivity) of phone/SM use qualify as MET regardless of direction/timeframe. This is not a demographic-only longitudinal case.
notes: C1 and C2 disagreed on FT_EC7 applicability. C2 argued it applies because predictors are individual traits. However, E16 explicitly states that individual-characteristic predictors of phone/SM use (including personality traits like impulsivity, reward sensitivity) result in MET status regardless of direction or timeframe, which exempts this from the FT_EC7 exclusion rule. The paper meets all inclusion criteria and should be included.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data from >3,000 young adolescents (11-14 years) at baseline and three assessments during COVID-19 pandemic (May-August 2020)

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper identifies 'more screen time' as a significant predictor of psychological distress in machine learning models. Screen time variables included as features in the comprehensive GBT and logistic regression models predicting mental health outcomes.

FT_IC3.evidence: Per E4 edge case rule: 'ML — coefficient reported → MET. SHAP/Gini/permutation only → NOT_MET.' Paper reports machine learning models (XGBoost, GBT) with feature importance via SHAP values. No traditional inferential test (regression coefficient, t-test, p-value) specific to screen time association is reported. Figure 2 shows top 20 features by mean absolute SHAP value for anxiety prediction, but these are importance rankings without direct effect estimates. Screen time is not reported with its own β, OR, or p-value coefficient.

FT_IC4.evidence: No extractable numeric coefficient (β, OR, p-value) for screen time→outcome association. SHAP importance values shown in figures (e.g., 'time spent browsing internet' ~0.10 SHAP value) are feature importance metrics, not traditional effect size statistics or test statistics for a specific screen-time association.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal machine learning study with prepandemic baseline and pandemic-period outcome assessments (anxiety, depression, stress, positive affect). Data-driven GBT and logistic regression classifiers trained to predict psychological distress.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in The Journal of Adolescent Health, 2022, DOI: 10.1016/j.jadohealth.2021.11.023. No evidence of duplication or preprint status.

FT_EC7_reason: Not applicable. Paper fails FT_IC3 and FT_IC4 criteria due to ML methodology (SHAP values only, no reported coefficients). Additionally, screen time is one predictor among many in a model predicting mental health outcomes (IV→DV direction), and the model includes individual psychological predictors (prepandemic internalizing symptoms, sleep problems, anxiety), not just demographics. FT-EC7 criteria not met.
notes: C1 correctly identified that SHAP-based ML feature importance does not qualify as a reportable coefficient per E4. This paper does not meet FT_IC3/FT_IC4 due to exclusive reliance on SHAP values without traditional effect estimates. Exclusion is appropriate.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data: 10,574 early adolescents at baseline (T1, ages 9-14 years from September 2016 to January 2021); 9,273 at follow-up (T2, ~2 years later)

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures adherence to 24-hour movement guidelines: ≥60 minutes physical activity, ≤2 hours screen time, 9-11 hours sleep. Screen time is operationalized as a composite component of the 24-hour guideline (meeting/not meeting ≤2 hours), NOT as a specific smartphone or social media variable. No breakdown of phone-specific use, social media hours, or platform-specific variables. Per FT-IC2 guidance: 'Generic total screen time or STS composite with no phone/SM breakdown → NOT_MET.'

FT_IC3.evidence: FT_IC2 criterion not met; FT_IC3 evaluation cannot proceed. (Paper does report linear mixed-effects models, but they test adherence categories rather than a specific phone/SM variable.)

FT_IC4.evidence: FT_IC2 criterion not met; no extractable coefficient for a phone/SM-specific variable.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis: cross-sectional (T1) and longitudinal (T1→T2, ~2 years) linear mixed-effects models examining associations between adherence to 24-hour movement guidelines and multiple health outcomes (cognition, BMI, psychosocial health, brain volumes)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in The Journal of Adolescent Health, Volume 72(3), March 2023, pages 460-470, DOI: 10.1016/j.jadohealth.2022.10.019

FT_EC7_reason: Not applicable. Paper fails FT_IC2 due to generic 'total screen time' composite (≤2 hours) with no smartphone/social-media-specific breakdown. FT_IC2 is a mandatory inclusion criterion.
notes: C1 correctly identified the exclusion. Generic screen time composite without phone/SM specificity does not meet FT_IC2 criteria. The paper frames screen time as part of integrated 24-hour movement recommendations but does not isolate or measure smartphone use, social media use, or platform-specific variables. Exclusion is appropriate.
